edges.description: Inoculation leads to draining regional lymphadenitis.
Some patients develop systemic organ involvement rather than isolated nodal disease.

nodes.description: In a subset of patients, CSD disseminates beyond regional lymph nodes, producing splenomegaly and hepatic or splenic microabscesses on imaging.

evidence.reference_title: Cat Scratch Disease: 9 Years of Experience at a Pediatric Center.
evidence.snippet: Among children with radiographic imaging, 20 of 55 (36.4%) had splenomegaly and 21 of 55 (38.1%) had splenic and/or hepatic microabscesses.

nodes.description: Scratching or biting by a colonized cat inoculates B. henselae into local tissue, setting up the first infectious trigger for CSD.

evidence.reference_title: Cat Scratch Disease: An Unusual Case of Right Inguinal Lymphadenitis Due to Bartonella henselae.
evidence.snippet: B. henselae is transmitted from cats to humans through scratching or biting when located on the cat's claws or oral cavity.

nodes.description: Draining lymph nodes enlarge and inflame, producing the classic regional lymphadenopathy syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Cat Scratch Disease: 9 Years of Experience at a Pediatric Center.
evidence.snippet: Although lymphadenopathy was present on physical examination in the majority of cases (78.8%), atypical presentations lacking lymphadenopathy were also common (63 of 304, 20.7%).
